title: Downed US pilot communicated with military hours after ejecting in Iran
content:
The weapons systems officer was reportedly wounded but still able to walk, and evaded Iranian forces in mountainous terrain for more than a day before being rescued.

The pilot was recovered within hours of the aircraft being shot down, while the second crew member remained missing until a longer search and extraction operation was completed. (Representative image)

US airman rescued after two days evading capture in Iran

Both crew members made contact after ejecting from the F-15

CIA ran deception campaign to mislead Iranian search teams Did our AI summary help?

The US airman whose fighter jet was shot down over Iranian territory reportedly made contact with the US military as early as Friday from behind enemy lines.

This development helped set in motion a complex rescue operation, according to the chief US security analyst at CNN, Jim Sciutto.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Sciutto, citing sources, said US forces established “early communications” with the stranded service member around noon Eastern Time on Friday, after which authorities immediately intensified efforts to locate and recover him.

“I was also told that he had been injured initially in the ejection, which of course presented other difficulties because they were concerned about getting him out, getting him out safely, being able to treat those injuries,” Sciutto said.

According to officials cited by Axios, both the pilot and the weapons systems officer were able to make contact via communications systems after ejecting from the F-15. The weapons systems officer was reportedly wounded but still able to walk, and evaded Iranian forces in mountainous terrain for more than a day before being rescued.

The pilot was recovered within hours of the aircraft being shot down, while the second crew member remained missing until a longer search and extraction operation was completed.

Sciutto described the broader mission as “remarkable,” noting that US aircraft conducting low-altitude search operations were exposed to potential hostile fire while attempting to locate the stranded airman.

Meanwhile, a senior administration official said the CIA carried out a deception campaign inside Iran prior to the rescue effort, claiming the agency “spread word inside Iran that U.S. forces had already found him and were moving him on the ground for exfiltration out of the country,” in an effort to mislead opposing search teams.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

As per officials, the airman evaded capture for nearly two days before being rescued in a coordinated operation involving intelligence, air power, and ground support.

Initial reports had indicated that during the initial rescue attempt for the first crew member, Iranian forces fired on US recovery aircraft, striking an A-10 Thunderbolt II attack aircraft and at least one rescue helicopter. The damaged A-10 was reportedly flown out of Iranian airspace before the pilot ejected, while officials said all rescue he

title: What two Earth photos, 54 years apart, tell us about space exploration
content:
From Apollo to Artemis, the images show not just how Earth looks from space, but how far human spaceflight has come.

Apollo 17’s 1972 photo by Harrison Schmitt shows Earth floating in space. (Image credit: X/NASA)

When NASA shared two images of Earth taken 54 years apart, what really stood out wasn’t how much the planet has changed, but how much everything around it has.

The first image goes back to 1972, taken during Apollo 17 by Harrison Schmitt. It’s one of those iconic shots, Earth hanging quietly in space, clear and complete. That image, often called the “Blue Marble,” ended up becoming one of the most widely shared photographs ever. For many people, it changed how they saw the planet, not as separate countries, but as one fragile, connected system.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Jump ahead to 2026, and the newer image comes from Artemis II, clicked by Reid Wiseman from the Orion spacecraft. At a glance, it looks almost the same. The same blue, the same continents. But if you look closer, there are subtle differences, like a faint green aurora glowing along the edge of the atmosphere, and even traces of light in space that earlier missions couldn’t capture as clearly.

What makes this comparison feel even more striking is the gap between the two missions.

The Apollo era was driven by urgency and competition. The goal was straightforward, get to the Moon and prove it could be done. And once that was achieved, human missions beyond Earth orbit simply stopped. For more than five decades, no astronaut travelled that far out again.

1972 2026 Apollo 17 Artemis II pic.twitter.com/wGc2wtY0e2 — NASA (@NASA) April 3, 2026

That’s what makes Artemis II different. This is the first time since 1972 that humans have gone this far from Earth, with the crew travelling over 100,000 miles out when these images were taken. But unlike Apollo, this mission isn’t about landing. It’s a test run, meant to prepare for longer, more sustained missions in the future, including a return to the Moon and eventually journeys to Mars.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

So while the image of Earth still feels familiar, calm and distant, the story behind it has shifted.

Back then, space travel was about reaching a milestone. Now, it’s about staying, returning and pushing further.

And that’s what these two photos really capture, not just the same planet seen from space, but two very different moments in how humans are learning to leave it.

title: Why a $20+ million space toilet matters more than it sounds
content:
The Artemis II toilet may sound like a quirky detail, but it reflects how modern space missions are being designed for longer, more practical human journeys.

Space toilets use controlled systems because nothing flows normally in microgravity. (Image credit: X)

Artemis II toilet costs USD23-30 million due to complex design

Microgravity requires advanced airflow and suction systems

Modern space toilets prioritize crew comfort and inclusivity Did our AI summary help?

When reports emerged that the toilet aboard Artemis II briefly malfunctioned after launch, the number attached to it caught everyone’s attention. At an estimated USD23-30 million, it sounded excessive for something so basic.

But in space, even the most ordinary functions become complex engineering problems.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

This toilet isn’t just a scaled-up version of what we use on Earth. In microgravity, nothing flows the way it normally would. Waste doesn’t fall, water doesn’t behave predictably and even small leaks or inefficiencies can become serious issues inside a sealed spacecraft. That means everything has to be carefully designed using airflow, suction and tightly controlled systems to make sure it works reliably.

The cost reflects that complexity. It includes years of research, testing in simulated zero-gravity conditions and designing systems that are lightweight, compact and extremely durable. Space hardware also has to meet very strict safety standards because repairs are not straightforward once a mission is underway.

There’s also a human factor that has become more important over time.

Earlier missions, including those during the Apollo era, did not have proper toilet systems. Astronauts had to rely on basic and often uncomfortable workarounds. That might have been manageable for shorter trips, but modern missions are longer and more demanding.

NASA has been trying to address that. The Artemis programme is designed with longer-duration space travel in mind, including future missions to the Moon and eventually Mars. That means crew comfort, hygiene and efficiency are no longer secondary concerns. They are part of mission design.

Feedback from astronauts, especially women, has played a role in shaping these systems. The newer designs are meant to be more ergonomic, easier to use and more inclusive, something earlier generations of space equipment did not fully account for.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

The glitch itself isn’t as dramatic as it sounds. Space systems go through a lot of testing, but once you’re actually in space, small issues do show up. That’s fairly normal, and most of them get fixed or adjusted as missions go along.

What it really shows is how space travel is changing. It’s not just about getting to orbit or landing on the Moon anymore. The focus is shifting to staying there longer and making it workable for humans.

And once you look at it that way, even something as basic as a toilet stops so

title: JASSM-ER stealth missile: The 600-mile long-range weapon whose entire inventory US is planning to deploy against Iran
content:
The Joint Air-to-Surface Standoff Missile–Extended Range, or JASSM-ER, is a stealthy, long-distance cruise missile built by Lockheed Martin for the US military.

US airmen transport a Joint Air-to-Surface Standoff Missile at Dyess Air Force Base, Texas. (Credit: Bloomberg)

US nearly depletes JASSM-ER missile stockpiles in Iran campaign

About 425 of 2,300 pre-war JASSM-ER missiles remain

Missile stockpiles may take years to rebuild at current output Did our AI summary help?

The United States is preparing to deploy a large share of its long-range JASSM-ER cruise missile inventory to its ongoing military operations against Iran, a move that is sharply reducing reserves originally intended for other regions.

Citing a source familiar with the development, a news report in the Economic Times citing a Bloomberg report said instructions were issued in late March to withdraw these high-value missiles—each costing roughly $1.5 million—from stockpiles in the Pacific. Additional supplies from the continental US and other locations are also being redirected to bases under US Central Command as well as to Fairford in the United Kingdom.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

The Joint Air-to-Surface Standoff Missile–Extended Range, or JASSM-ER, is a stealthy, long-distance cruise missile built by Lockheed Martin for the US military. It is designed to hit strategic targets deep within hostile territory while bypassing sophisticated air defence systems.

With a strike range exceeding 600 miles, the weapon allows aircraft to engage targets from a safe distance, without entering heavily defended zones. It is commonly launched from bombers such as the B-1B Lancer and B-52 Stratofortress, as well as from certain fighter aircraft.

As The Economic Times explains, the JASSM-ER is an upgraded version of the original JASSM, which has a much shorter reach of about 250 miles. Its stealth profile and precision targeting capabilities make it particularly effective against fortified or high-risk objectives, reducing the need for aircraft to operate within contested airspace.

Unlike the Tomahawk missile, which is typically fired from naval platforms, the JASSM-ER is air-launched, offering greater flexibility in how and where it can be deployed.

Before the current conflict with Iran, the US was estimated to have a stockpile of around 2,300 JASSM-ER missiles. However, extensive operational use and redeployment have significantly reduced that number.

Following recent allocations, only about 425 missiles are expected to remain available for use, with an additional portion considered unserviceable due to technical limitations. At this level, the remaining inventory would be sufficient for only a limited number of bomber sorties.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

According to Economic Times, since 2009, the US has funded the acquisition of more than 6,200 JASSM variants in total, although production of the original model ended roughly a decade ago. Th

title: Fires at Abu Dhabi petrochemical plant, Kuwait power units shut after Iran attacks
content:
Iran’s central military command dismissed a U.S. ultimatum threatening severe consequences if Tehran did not agree to a peace deal, with a senior commander calling it “a helpless, nervous, unbalanced and stupid action.”

Representative image

Debris from interception sparks fires at Abu Dhabi plant

Kuwait and Bahrain report damage to utilities, no casualties

Iran intensifies aerial attacks, Gulf states on high alert Did our AI summary help?

Authorities in Abu Dhabi on Sunday said emergency teams were tackling blazes at a major petrochemical complex after debris fell during air defence interceptions, as Iran intensified its aerial offensive across the Gulf, reported AFP.

“Authorities are responding to multiple fires in Borouge petrochemicals plant, caused by falling debris following successful interceptions by air defence systems,” the emirate’s media office said. Officials added that operations at the site were halted immediately to allow for a damage assessment, noting that no casualties had been reported.

The incident came as the United Arab Emirates’ defence ministry said it was countering incoming missile and drone attacks. Iran had earlier indicated it was targeting the country’s “aluminium industries,” underscoring a widening scope of strikes on strategic infrastructure in the region.

Elsewhere in the Gulf, Kuwait reported damage to critical utilities following drone strikes. The country’s electricity and water ministry said two power generation and desalination facilities were affected, leading to operational disruptions. The attack resulted in “the shutdown of two electricity generating units,” the ministry said, adding that there were no deaths or injuries.

Kuwait’s military had earlier stated that its air defence systems were actively intercepting missiles and drones, as the country faced continued aerial threats. A separate incident on Saturday evening caused “significant” damage to a government building in Kuwait City, according to a finance ministry statement, which also confirmed that no injuries were recorded.

The developments highlight the growing regional impact of the escalating conflict, with key economic and infrastructure sites increasingly drawn into the crossfire. While authorities in both Abu Dhabi and Kuwait emphasised the absence of casualties, the attacks have disrupted operations and raised concerns about the resilience of critical facilities.

Across the Gulf, governments have moved to contain damage from the strikes. In Bahrain, the interior ministry said civil defence teams had brought a fire under control at an unspecified facility after what it described as “Iranian aggression,” again reporting no fatalities.

The broader escalation has also extended beyond the Gulf. Israel said it was targeted by missiles launched from Iran on Sunday, while Iranian media reported casualties in the country’s northwest following strikes attributed to U.S. and Israeli forces.

Amid the intensifying confrontation, rhetoric ha

title: Fire erupts at Bapco oil storage facility in Bahrain after Iranian airstrikes | Watch
content:
The Interior Ministry said civil defence teams were deployed immediately after the incident, adding that “measures were taken to control a fire that broke out as a result of the Iranian aggression targeting one of the facilities.”

Earlier, on March 9, Bapco had declared force majeure on its operations following an attack on its refinery complex.

Fire at Bapco oil facility in Bahrain after Iranian airstrike

Blaze extinguished, no casualties reported, damage assessed

Domestic fuel supply remains secure despite disruption Did our AI summary help?

A fire broke out at an oil storage facility operated by Bapco Energies in Bahrain on Sunday following an Iranian airstrike. The blaze has since been brought under control and extinguished, confirmed officials.

The Interior Ministry said civil defence teams were deployed immediately after the incident, adding that “measures were taken to control a fire that broke out as a result of the Iranian aggression targeting one of the facilities.”

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Sirens were sounded in the area, with residents urged to remain calm and move to nearby safe locations as emergency crews responded.

Massive fire erupted at the Bapco oil refinery in Bahrain after it was hit by missiles and explosive drones. Follow: https://t.co/mLGcUTS2eipic.twitter.com/kGh30XzrKg — Press TV 🔻 (@PressTV) April 5, 2026

The state-run Bahrain News Agency later reported that the fire, which affected a warehouse linked to the energy company, had been fully extinguished. Authorities confirmed that no casualties were reported, while damage assessments at the site are currently underway.

Earlier, officials said the blaze had broken out at a Bapco facility without initially disclosing the exact location. Emergency teams were deployed to prevent the fire from spreading to other parts of the installation.

Earlier, on March 9, Bapco had declared force majeure on its operations following an attack on its refinery complex, further highlighting the growing disruption to energy infrastructure amid escalating regional tensions.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

The company’s Sitra refinery, one of its key assets with a capacity of around 380,000 barrels per day, has also faced operational strain during the conflict.

Despite the disruption, Bapco officials have said domestic fuel supply remains secure, noting that contingency measures are in place to ensure continued availability of petroleum products in the local market and exports across the Middle East and Asia.

Authorities have not yet released full details on the scale of damage from the latest incident or confirmed the extent of operational impact.

title: Trump says F-15E rescue of pilots a ‘first in military memory’: A look at past US missions
content:
The remark followed reports of two American aviators recovered after incidents in or near Iran

President Donald Trump said Sunday that US forces had safely recovered a second airman downed in Iran, calling it "one of the most daring Search and Rescue Operations in U.S. History".

Trump's claim of unprecedented rescues is historically inaccurate

US military has conducted similar deep rescues in past conflicts

Vietnam, Korea, and later wars saw separate pilot recoveries Did our AI summary help?

A claim by US President Donald Trump that a recent operation marked “the first time in military memory that two US pilots have been rescued, separately, deep in enemy territory” draws attention towards historical records of earlier US combat search and rescue missions.

The remark followed reports of two American aviators recovered after incidents in or near Iran. According to reports, one crew member from a downed F-15E and another pilot, likely flying an A-10 aircraft, were both extracted in separate efforts without reported fatalities in those specific missions. The operations themselves are being described as complex and high-risk.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Claim of two rescues in hostile territory

While the underlying events -- two separate rescues in hostile territory -- are supported by reporting, the sweeping nature of the statement raises questions. Describing the episode as unprecedented “in military memory” is difficult to sustain when measured against decades of US military history.

History shows repeated precedents

Military records indicate that US forces have carried out numerous deep-recovery missions for downed aircrew across multiple conflicts.

During World War II, early forms of combat search and rescue were developed, including missions behind enemy lines. By 1944, helicopter evacuations in Burma had already demonstrated the feasibility of extracting personnel from hostile territory, according to Smithsonian's Air & Space Magazine.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

According to a U.S. Department of Defense publication, 'That Others May Live: USAF Air Rescue in Korea', similar patterns continued during the Korean War, where American forces conducted high-risk recoveries of pilots shot down over North Korea. These were often individual missions aimed at retrieving isolated crew members, sometimes far from friendly lines.

Vietnam war: A benchmark for CSAR operations

The Vietnam War stands out as a defining period for such missions. Often described as the most intensive era for combat search and rescue, it saw repeated efforts to retrieve downed aviators under heavy fire.

One of the most cited examples is the 1972 Bat 21 mission. Lt. Col. Iceal Hambleton, whose aircraft was downed in a heavily defended region, was eventually rescued after a prolonged and complex operation involving extensive air and ground coordination. Other related recoveries from the same period were also carried out separately

title: Iranian media says IRGC downed US C-130 in Isfahan, five killed during search as Trump announces pilot rescue
content:
Tasnim news agency quoted the Guards as saying, "An American enemy aircraft that was searching for the pilot of a downed fighter jet was destroyed by the fighters of Islam in the southern region of Isfahan."

This handout photo provided by Iran's Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) official website Sepah News on April 5, 2026 reportedly shows the wreckage and remains of targeted and crashed aircraft in central Iran. Pic: AFP

Iran says police commandos destroyed US C-130 in rescue attempt

Trump says US rescued two pilots from Iran in risky operations

Strikes on Iran spark fears for global energy supplies Did our AI summary help?

Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps said a US aircraft linked to a search effort was destroyed, Iranian media reported Sunday, amid competing narratives over a US rescue mission inside Iran.

Fars news agency said the aircraft, identified as a C-130, was brought down by a police commando unit called the Faraj Rangers. It claimed the plane was “refuelling the evil invaders of the sacred land of the homeland”.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Overnight strikes in southwestern Iran killed five people during efforts to recover a missing US crewmember from a downed fighter jet, Iranian media reported on Sunday.

“Five people were martyred in last night’s attack on the Kouh-e Siah area” in Kohgiluyeh and Boyer-Ahmad province, provincial official Iraj Kazemijou was quoted as saying by the Tasnim news agency.

He added that the deaths occurred as the United States was searching for “the pilot or pilots” of the crashed aircraft.

This handout photo provided by Iran's Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) official website Sepah News on April 5, 2026 shows smoke billowing reportedly from the site of targeted and crashed aircraft in central Iran. AFP

In a post on Truth Social, Trump said the rescued service member was located deep inside hostile territory. "This brave Warrior was behind enemy lines in the treacherous mountains of Iran, being hunted down by our enemies," he said, adding, "He sustained injuries, but he will be just fine."

Trump also indicated that another pilot had been recovered earlier, but the operation had not been disclosed at the time. "This miraculous Search and Rescue Operation comes in addition to a successful rescue of another brave Pilot, yesterday, which we did not confirm, because we did not want to jeopardize our second rescue operation."

title: Behind Enemy Lines: How US pulled off a 'near-impossible' pilot rescue mission inside hostile Iran
content:
The operation involved hundreds of special forces troops, dozens of aircraft and helicopters, and integrated intelligence support across multiple domains

The operation involved hundreds of special forces troops, dozens of aircraft and helicopters, and integrated intelligence support across multiple domains. (File photo)

US airman rescued after F-15E jet shot down over Iran

US forces raced Iranian troops to extract the stranded airman

All US personnel evacuated safely after complex rescue mission Did our AI summary help?

A US fighter jet shoots out of the sky over hostile territory. Two crew members eject into the darkness. One of them quickly disappears into rugged mountains while enemy forces close in from all sides.

Except there are no cameras rolling. This is real-world warfare.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Because what followed was not fiction; it was a sprawling, high-risk special operations mission deep inside conflict-struck Iran that military leaders would later describe as "one of the most challenging and complex in the history of US special operations".

How it all began...

The operation began when an F-15E fighter jet was struck by Iranian fire over Iranian territory earlier this week, forcing both crew members to eject.

One pilot was recovered within hours. The second crew member, a weapons systems officer (WSO), survived but was injured and stranded in mountainous terrain with only minimal survival gear.

Despite the hostile environment, the airman was not entirely isolated. According to officials, he carried a beacon and a secure communications device that continuously transmitted his position back to US command centres.

The WSО, reportedly injured but mobile, used SERE (Survival, Evasion, Resistance and Escape) training to evade capture, moving away from the wreckage, taking cover on elevated ground, and activating the emergency beacon.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

That tracking capability became the backbone of the rescue effort.

The 'race' between two militaries

What followed was not just a rescue mission; it was a race. Against time and all odds.

US forces quickly moved to locate and extract the airman while Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) launched parallel search efforts in the same mountainous region of southwest Iran.

Officials cited by Axios said both sides were closing in on the same zone, turning the operation into a high-stakes contest of speed, intelligence, and firepower.

To prevent Iranian forces from reaching the survivor, US aircraft carried out strikes on approaching convoys. One official described it as a “hail of heavy fire” used to clear access routes and secure the extraction corridor.

At the same time, intelligence assets, including cyber and space support, helped guide real-time positioning as commanders monitored the operation from Washington, including from the White House Situation Room, where President Donald Trump reportedly followed dev

title: Prediction market taking bets on search for missing US military service member shut down- Moneycontrol.com
content:
Polymarket has recently been coming under increasing scrutiny, including calls for tougher regulation of prediction markets

The criticism has mounted after some of its users made substantial bets ahead of the war in Iran and the military action in Venezuela earlier this year.

A prediction market accepting bets on the search for a US military service member still missing from a fighter jet shot down in the Iran war Friday has been shut down by Polymarket for violating its standards.

Polymarket, which has turned prediction markets into an increasingly popular phenomenon alongside rival operator Kalshi, disclosed it blocked the wagering on the search for the missing military member about two hours after US Rep Seth Moulton posted a note of outrage on social media.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

“This is DISGUSTING,” wrote Moulton, a Democrat from Massachusetts who was in combat in the Middle East while serving in the Marines.

In its response on the X platform, Polymarket said Friday it had removed the market cited by Moulton and was investigating how it slipped through the safeguards set up to protect its integrity standards.

Polymarket has recently been coming under increasing scrutiny, including calls for tougher regulation of prediction markets.

The criticism has mounted after some of its users made substantial bets ahead of the war in Iran and the military action in Venezuela earlier this year.

Police forcibly dispersed anti-war protest in Tel Aviv

Several hundred people attended the demonstration at Habima Square in Tel Aviv calling for an end to the war, in between sirens warning of incoming missiles from Iran and from Yemen.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Israeli wartime restrictions currently limit gatherings in public areas to 150 people. But in response to an appeal, supreme court justices on Saturday evening issued an interim ruling saying at least 600 people would be allowed to gather at Habima Square and 150 at each of several other locations across the country.

At around 8 pm, police declared the demonstration unlawful, claiming there were “hundreds more than the court ordered” at the protest. Police officers forcibly removed protesters from the square and arrested at least 17.

Argentina says Iran's top diplomat expelled

Argentina's Foreign Affairs Ministry said Saturday it had expelled Iran's top diplomat as tensions increase between Iran's theocratic regime and Argentina's libertarian government, which is closely aligned with the Trump administration.

In a message on X, Argentina's Foreign Affairs Minister Pablo Quirno said Iran's Charge D'Affaires, Mohsen Soltani Tehrani, had left the country “in compliance” with a resolution issued Thursday that gave the Iranian diplomat 48 hours to leave the South American country.

Tensions between both nations intensified earlier this week after Argentina declared Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps a terrorist organisation.

title: 'Battle-tested generals refuse to be yes-men for TV host': Iran's Parliament Speaker slams Trump admin
content:
The comments come as US Secretary of War Pete Hegseth forced out two army generals in a major rejig after vowing for regime change in the Iran war, the Wall Street Journal reported

Iran has repeatedly threatened to close or restrict shipping through the Strait, where roughly 20% of the world's oil passes

Iran's Parliament Speaker MB Ghalibaf, on Sunday, praised battle-tested generals for prioritising national interests over external pressures, amidst tensions between the US and Iran.

In a post on X, he highlighted that the US grapples with decisions on supporting Israel and managing oil prices, potentially impacting American taxpayers in the long term.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

He said, "SITREP: Battle-tested generals refuse to be yes-men for a TV host selling out American lives for Israel's delusions. That's the short-term price. Long-term cost? American taxpayers paying at the pump for decades. Real LEGACY."

Ghalibaf is suggesting a rift between professional US military "generals" and the political appointees (like Hegseth) who he claims are "selling out" American interests for Israel's military goals. Ghalibaf is hinting at Iran's ability to disrupt global energy supplies.

Iran has repeatedly threatened to close or restrict shipping through the Strait, where roughly 20% of the world's oil passes. He is arguing that while the U.S. might see "short-term" military wins, the "long-term cost" for American taxpayers will be decades of high energy prices and economic instability due to the destruction of Middle Eastern energy infrastructure.

The comments come as US Secretary of War Pete Hegseth forced out two army generals in a major rejig after vowing for regime change in the Iran war, the Wall Street Journal reported.

General David Hodne, who became the head of the service's Training and Transformation Command in October, and Major General William Green Jr., Chief of Army Chaplains, were removed after General Randy George, the Army's chief of staff, as reported by the Wall Street Journal.

The removal comes late in a series of clashes between the Pentagon chief and the service's senior leadership. George has been asked to retire immediately, even though he was expected to hold the office for more than another year, until the fall of 2027, and complete what is typically a four-year assignment as a member of the Joint Chiefs of Staff.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Meanwhile, on March 28, demonstrators hit the streets of cities across the United States for the first "No Kings" protest since the joint US and Israeli war against Iran, as reported by Al Jazeera.

According to the "No Kings" website, more than 3,300 events were planned across all 50 states, with large crowds expected in cities such as New York, Los Angeles and Washington, DC. Parallel events happened internationally in cities such as Rome, Paris, and Berlin.

title: US inflation seen spiking in first snapshot since Iran war

Combined with signs of stabilization in the US labor market, stubborn price pressures along with new inflation risks stemming from war in the Middle East help explain why the Fed may struggle to lower interest rates this year.

US inflation to surge amid Iran war and soaring gas prices

Fed may struggle to lower rates amid new inflation risks

Central banks worldwide likely to keep policy steady this week Did our AI summary help?

The sudden increase in US gasoline prices felt by American consumers is set to be on full display in key inflation data due out this coming week.

Economists are penciling in a 1% increase in the consumer price index for March — the sharpest one-month advance since 2022 — after the Iran war pushed gas prices at the pump up by about $1 per gallon.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

At the same time, the core CPI, excluding energy and food, probably rose 0.3% from a month earlier, according to a Bloomberg survey ahead of the Bureau of Labor Statistics report due Friday.

A day ahead of the CPI, the Federal Reserve’s preferred gauge of inflation will offer a snapshot of pre-war price pressures. Economists see the so-called core personal consumption expenditures (PCE) price index, which excludes food and energy, having risen by 0.4% for a third month in February, suggesting progress toward tamer inflation was stalling even before the conflict.

Combined with signs of stabilization in the US labor market, stubborn price pressures along with new inflation risks stemming from war in the Middle East help explain why the Fed may struggle to lower interest rates this year.

The mid-week release of minutes from the central bank’s March policy meeting may shed light on officials’ concerns about inflation or the potential economic impacts stemming from the Iran conflict and related disruptions to energy and other commodity flows.

In addition to the PCE price data, the Bureau of Economic Analysis’ report will include figures on personal spending as well as incomes. Economists expect a modest increase in inflation-adjusted spending.

Other reports in the coming week include the Institute for Supply Management’s March services activity index, due on Monday. And on Friday, the University of Michigan will issue its preliminary April consumer sentiment index.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

In Canada, the March labor force survey will offer a first look at how surging energy costs may be filtering through to job growth and unemployment. Economists expect the jobless rate to tick up to 6.8%.

Elsewhere, central banks from Poland to India and New Zealand may keep policy steady as they monitor events in the Middle East, while inflation gauges from China to Latin America will point to the impact on living costs.

Asia

Asia gets three rate decisions this week, with the focus falling on how

title: Why first F-15E pilot rescue was kept secret? Trump reveals claiming air dominance over Iranian skies
content:
Trump disclosed that another pilot had already been rescued a day earlier, but officials chose not to make that information public at the time.

US President Donald Trump

Second F-15E crew member rescued after dramatic firefight

Earlier pilot rescue kept secret to protect ongoing mission

Both rescues completed with no American casualties Did our AI summary help?

US President Donald Trump has confirmed that a second crew member from a downed F-15E fighter jet has been recovered, while also revealing that an earlier rescue was deliberately kept undisclosed to avoid jeopardizing the ongoing mission.

In a post on Truth Social, Trump announced the latest development, declaring, “WE GOT HIM!” and describing the effort as “one of the most daring Search and Rescue Operations in U.S. History.” He said the rescued officer, identified only as a senior-ranking crew member, is now “SAFE and SOUND” despite sustaining injuries.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Trump disclosed that another pilot had already been rescued a day earlier, but officials chose not to make that information public at the time. “This miraculous Search and Rescue Operation comes in addition to a successful rescue of another brave Pilot, yesterday, which we did not confirm, because we did not want to jeopardize our second rescue operation,” he wrote.

The decision to withhold details underscores the risks surrounding the second mission, which was still underway when the first recovery occurred. According to reporting by Al Jazeera, the second crew member had been evading Iranian forces for nearly two days after the aircraft was shot down. The rescue unfolded under dangerous conditions, with correspondents describing a “dramatic firefight” during the operation.

While Trump’s statement suggested a completed and successful extraction, reporting from Al Jazeera indicated the situation remained fluid. A US official told the network that although the missing crew member had been located and recovered, the operation was not fully complete. The rescue team still needed to exit Iranian territory safely, meaning both the airman and the personnel involved could remain at risk.

“There was a fierce firefight,” Al Jazeera reported, citing a US government source, adding that the mission was “ongoing” and that safe exfiltration had not yet been confirmed at that stage.

Trump framed the dual rescues as a demonstration of US military strength, emphasizing that both missions were carried out without casualties. “The fact that we were able to pull off both of these operations, without a SINGLE American killed, or even wounded, just proves once again, that we have achieved overwhelming Air Dominance and Superiority over the Iranian skies,” he said.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

The contrasting accounts highlight both the complexity of the operation and the sensitivity of releasing information during active military engagements, particularly when personnel remain inside

title: After ouster, US Army chief Randy George says soldiers need 'courageous leaders of character'
content:
According to CBS News, one source indicated that US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth is seeking leadership aligned with his and President Donald Trump’s strategic direction for the Army.

Ousted General Randy George, Chief of Staff of the U.S. Army (Reuters)

Gen. Randy George retires as Army chief after Hegseth order

Gen. Christopher LaNeve named acting Army chief

Kuwait intercepts missiles amid Iran's retaliatory strikes Did our AI summary help?

General Randy George, who has been removed as US Army chief of staff, told senior Pentagon officials in a farewell communication that soldiers must be led by “courageous leaders of character,” following a directive from Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth for him to step aside and retire immediately.

According to CBS News, one source indicated that Hegseth is seeking leadership aligned with his and President Donald Trump’s strategic direction for the Army. The email attributed to George, later verified by CBS News, was circulated among top Army leadership, including Undersecretary Driscoll, assistant secretaries, and multiple senior-ranking officers.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Reflecting on his tenure, George wrote, “It has been the greatest privilege to serve beside you and lead Soldiers in support of our country.” He urged continuity in operational focus, saying, “I know you'll all continue to stay laser-focused on the mission, continue innovating, and relentlessly cut through the bureaucracy to get our warfighters what they need to win on the modern battlefield.”

Emphasizing standards of leadership, he added, “Our soldiers are truly the best in the world – they deserve tough training and courageous leaders of character. I have no doubt you will all continue to lead with courage, character, and grit.”

George, who previously served as senior military assistant to Defense Secretary Lloyd Austin between 2021 and 2022, had taken over as Army chief in 2023, a role that typically spans four years.

Confirming his departure, Pentagon spokesperson Sean Parnell said George “will be retiring from his position as the 41st Chief of Staff of the Army effective immediately,” adding that the department appreciates his “decades of service to our nation” and wishes him well.

In the interim, Gen. Christopher LaNeve, currently vice chief of staff and a former military aide to Hegseth, has been named acting Army chief.

Separately, tensions escalated in the Gulf region. Kuwait’s military reported that its air defenses were actively intercepting missiles and drones as Iran continued retaliatory operations following U.S.-Israeli strikes. A drone strike on Saturday caused “significant” damage to a government building in Kuwait City, though no casualties were reported.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Israel also came under attack, with its military stating that missiles were launched from Iran on Sunday.

Meanwhile, Iranian media reported that strikes in Ardabil province near the Azerbaij

title: US rescues second F-15E crew member in Iran in 'daring' mission
content:
The rescued crew member, whose identity has not been disclosed, was one of two aircrew aboard the F-15E Strike Eagle shot down by Iranian forces on Friday.

A specialised commando unit conducted the operation with air cover, and all personnel involved were said to have exited Iranian airspace safely. (Representative image)

US forces rescued a stranded American airman in Iran

The operation was a high-risk mission with air support

All personnel exited Iranian airspace safely Did our AI summary help?

US forces have rescued the second American airman stranded in Iran in a high-risk operation early, after the crew member spent more than 24 hours inside hostile territory, according to US officials.

The rescue was carried out by a specialised commando unit with air support, in what officials described as a “daring” mission.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

All personnel involved in the operation have safely exited Iranian airspace, they said.

“WE GOT HIM!” an elated US President Donald Trump announced in a statement Sunday morning, adding that the officer, described as a “highly respected Colonel,” is now “SAFE and SOUND.”

The aviator, whose identity has not yet been disclosed, was one of two crew members aboard an F-15E Strike Eagle fighter jet that was shot down by Iranian forces on Friday. The second crew member was rescued shortly after the crash and is reported to be safe and receiving medical attention.

The operation followed an intensive search effort launched immediately after the aircraft went down, with US forces racing to locate and extract the missing airman from difficult terrain.

According to The Wall Street Journal, which cited two US officials, the extraction involved a coordinated assault supported by air cover.

Meanwhile, according to Iranian state media, the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps said it had “destroyed” the US aircraft that was taking part in operations to locate the downed F-15E crew member, reported AFP.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

"An American enemy aircraft that was searching for the pilot of a downed fighter jet was destroyed by the fighters of Islam in the southern region of Isfahan," the Tasnim news agency cited the Guards.

However, there has been no immediate confirmation from Washington on this.

The successful rescue comes less than two days before Trump’s deadline for Iran to negotiate a deal with the U.S. or reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

title: Satellite firm Planet Labs to indefinitely withhold Iran war images- Moneycontrol.com
content:
California-based Planet Labs announced the decision in an email to customers and said the US government had asked all satellite imagery providers to indefinitely withhold images of the conflict region

A satellite image shows Iran's Law Enforcement Command (FARAJA) in Tehran, Iran, March 3, 2026, amid the U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran. 2026 Planet Labs PBC/Handout via REUTERS/File Photo

Planet Labs to indefinitely halt satellite imagery of Iran

US orders providers to limit imagery of conflict regions

Images released individually for urgent or public-interest needs Did our AI summary help?

Satellite imaging firm Planet Labs said on Saturday it will indefinitely withhold visuals of Iran and the region of conflict in the Middle East to comply with a request from the U.S. government.

California-based Planet Labs announced the decision in an email to customers and said the U.S. government had asked all satellite imagery providers to indefinitely withhold images of the conflict region.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

The restriction expands upon a 14-day delay on imagery of the Middle East that Planet Labs imposed last month, a move the firm said was meant to prevent adversaries from using it to attack the U.S. and its allies.

Planet Labs said it will withhold imagery dating back to March 9 and that it expects the policy to remain in effect until the conflict ends.

The war began when the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran on February 28, and the conflict spread in the region when Tehran responded by launching its own attacks on Israel and U.S. bases in Gulf states, including Saudi Arabia, Kuwait and Bahrain.

Military uses of satellite technology include target identification, weapons guidance, missile tracking and communications. Some space specialists say Iran could be accessing commercial imagery, including pictures obtained via U.S. adversaries. Satellite images also help journalists and academicians studying hard-to-reach places.

Planet Labs, which operates a large fleet of Earth-imaging satellites and sells frequently updated images to governments, companies and media, did not respond to a request for further comment.

The Pentagon said it does not comment on intelligence-related matters.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Planet Labs said in its email to customers that it would switch to a "managed distribution of images" deemed not to pose a risk to safety. Under a new system, Planet Labs will release imagery on a case-by-case basis for urgent, mission-critical requirements or in the public interest.

"These are extraordinary circumstances, and we are doing all we can to balance the needs of all our stakeholders," the firm said.

One commercial provider, Vantor, formerly Maxar Technologies, told Reuters that it was not contacted by the U.S. government. Vantor for years has reserved the right to "implement enhanced access controls during times of geopolitical conflict" and currently has applied them for parts of

title: Why Iran FM Abbas Araghchi warns Bushehr nuclear power plant strikes could trigger fallout beyond borders | Explained
content:
Iran’s foreign minister Abbas Araghchi's remarks underscored a key geographic reality: Bushehr’s coastal placement makes neighbouring Arab states more immediately exposed than Iran’s own political centre.

Bushehr nuclear power plant (AP file photo)

Bushehr is Iran's only operational civilian nuclear power plant.

Recent strikes near Bushehr raise regional safety concerns.

Bushehr's coastal location puts Gulf cities at greater risk. Did our AI summary help?

The Bushehr nuclear power plant sits on Iran’s southern coastline along the Persian Gulf, a geography that makes it strategically significant -- and unusually sensitive in times of conflict.

It is the country’s only operational civilian nuclear power facility, positioned closer to Gulf capitals like Kuwait City and Doha than to Tehran, which lies over 750 kilometres away. This proximity has long worried neighbouring states, especially given seismic activity in the region and the potential consequences of any nuclear incident.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Recent strikes near the facility have once again brought global attention to its location and vulnerability. According to Iranian state media, a strike on Saturday killed a security guard. It was the fourth such incident since late February, and it prompted Russia -- whose technicians help operate the plant -- to begin evacuating 198 workers linked to Rosatom.

Iran’s foreign minister Abbas Araghchi warned that continued attacks could have catastrophic regional consequences, stating that radioactive fallout would “end life in GCC (Gulf Cooperation Council) capitals, not Tehran.”

His remarks underscored a key geographic reality: Bushehr’s coastal placement makes neighbouring Arab states more immediately exposed than Iran’s own political centre.

Origins under the Shah and decades of delays

The story of Bushehr dates back half a century, AFP reported. Construction began in 1975 during the rule of the Shah, when Iran awarded the project to Germany’s Siemens as part of a broader push toward modernisation.

But the 1979 Islamic Revolution abruptly halted progress, and the subsequent Iran-Iraq War further derailed the project, leaving the partially built facility damaged and abandoned.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Iran revived the plan in the late 1980s, seeking to diversify its energy mix despite being a major oil and gas producer. The goal was to reduce domestic reliance on fossil fuels and free up more hydrocarbons for export. However, geopolitical pressures intervened again. The German government persuaded Siemens to withdraw, citing concerns about nuclear proliferation.

It was only in 1995 that the project regained momentum, when Russia stepped in to take over construction. The agreement envisioned a 1,000-megawatt pressurized water reactor that would be operational by 1999. Instead, technical setbacks, financial disputes, and political complications delayed completion by more than a decade. The plan

title: AI at war: Five things to know about Project Maven- Moneycontrol.com
content:
Project Maven is the Pentagon's flagship artificial intelligence program, launched in 2017 as a narrow experiment to help military analysts make sense of the torrent of drone footage pouring in from conflict zones.

Operators were drowning in imagery, searching frame by frame for objects of interest that might appear for only a moment before vanishing. Maven was built to find the needle in the haystack.

Project Maven is the Pentagon’s key AI for battlefield targeting

Palantir is now Maven's primary technology contractor.

Maven sped up US strikes, hitting 500 targets per day Did our AI summary help?

A Pentagon AI program called Project Maven is at the center of the US strikes against Iran and potentially one of the most consequential transformations of modern warfare.

- What is it? -

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Project Maven is the Pentagon's flagship artificial intelligence program, launched in 2017 as a narrow experiment to help military analysts make sense of the torrent of drone footage pouring in from conflict zones.

Operators were drowning in imagery, searching frame by frame for objects of interest that might appear for only a moment before vanishing. Maven was built to find the needle in the haystack.

Eight years later, the program has evolved into something far more expansive: an AI-assisted targeting and battlefield management system that has vastly accelerated what is known in war-making as the kill chain -- the process from initial detection to destruction.

- How does it work? -

Maven functions like both the air traffic control of battle and its cockpit.

Aalok Mehta, director of the CSIS Wadhwani AI Center, described the system as "essentially an overlay" that fuses sensor data, enemy troop intelligence, satellite imagery, and information on troop deployment.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

In practice, that means rapidly scanning satellite feeds to detect troop movements or identify targets, while also "taking a snapshot of the operational theater" to determine the best course of action for striking a specific target.

In a recent demonstration posted online, a Pentagon official described how Maven "magically" turns an observed threat into a targeting workflow, weighing available assets and presenting a commander with options.

The emergence of ChatGPT was another leap forward, broadening the use of the technology to a far greater range of users who can interact with Maven in natural language.

For now, this capability is supplied by Anthropic's Claude — though that arrangement is coming to a bitter end after the Pentagon bristled at the AI lab's demand that its model not be used for fully automated strikes or the tracking of US citizens.

- Why did Google say no? -

The ethical question was a factor in Maven's early years, when Google was the program's original AI contractor.

In 2018, more than 3,000 employees signed an open letter protesting the company's involvement, arguing that the c

title: HLS: Drone strike hits Kuwait Petroleum HQ as Iran widens Gulf attacks
content:
The escalation came as Iran launched fresh missile and drone attacks on Israel and Kuwait early Sunday, in response to ongoing US-Israeli strikes.

Hours before the attack, Iran’s semi-official Fars news agency announced an updated “target list” that included electrical, water and steam infrastructure, in addition to the oil, natural gas and chemical assets that already have been under fire.

Drone strike hit Kuwait Petroleum HQ, causing fire and evacuation

Iran intensified attacks on Gulf energy infrastructure Sunday

Conflict disrupts energy markets, raising fears of regional war Did our AI summary help?

A drone strike hit the headquarters of Kuwait Petroleum Corporation early Sunday, sparking a fire. The building, which also houses the emirate’s oil ministry, was evacuated as Iran stepped up attacks on Gulf energy infrastructure.

Firefighting teams were deployed to the site, while officials said damage assessments were underway.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

“The oil sector leadership is closely monitoring the assessment of damages… while taking all necessary measures to ensure the safety of personnel and secure the site,” the company said in a statement.

The strike followed a series of recent aerial attacks targeting Kuwait’s key energy facilities, including the Mina Al-Ahmadi and Mina Abdullah refineries, as well as repeated strikes on the country’s airport.

Hours before the latest attack, Iran’s semi-official Fars news agency published an updated “target list” that expanded beyond oil and gas facilities to include electricity, water and steam infrastructure. The list also named industrial assets such as Kuwait’s PIC fertilizer and polymer manufacturer.

The escalation came as Iran launched fresh missile and drone attacks on Israel and Kuwait early Sunday, in response to ongoing US-Israeli strikes.

Both countries said their air defence systems were engaged in intercepting incoming threats.

The conflict, now over a month old, has spread across the Middle East, disrupting global energy markets and threatening shipping through the critical Strait of Hormuz.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

A US- or Israeli-linked strike on a petrochemical hub in southwestern Iran killed at least five people on Saturday, according to local officials.

US President Donald Trump renewed pressure on Tehran, warning it had 48 hours to agree to a deal or face severe consequences.

Iran’s central military command rejected the ultimatum, with General Ali Abdollahi Aliabadi calling it a “helpless, nervous, unbalanced and stupid action,” and warning in response that “the gates of hell will open.”

Strikes have increasingly targeted economic and industrial infrastructure across the region. US-Israeli attacks hit a cement plant and a trade terminal on the Iran-Iraq border, while Iran retaliated with drone and missile strikes across the Gulf.

Falling debris from intercepted drones injured four people in Bahrain, while in Dubai, two b

title: How Iran’s downing of US jets punctures Trump’s aura of invincibility
content:
Trump has repeatedly claimed dominance over Iranian airspace and used maximalist rhetoric to suggest the US has won

Trump has dangled the possibility of the war ending shortly with a claim of victory as a means to calm markets and reassure nervous members of Congress

Iran downs US jet, casting doubt on Trump’s victory claims

Trump faces political backlash as Americans oppose the Iran war.

Loss of US aircraft raises doubts about Trump's air supremacy. Did our AI summary help?

The downing of a US aircraft and the dayslong search for a missing crew member has pierced the aura of invincibility that President Donald Trump has sought to project as he tries to stave off the increasing political risks of the Iran war he started five weeks ago alongside Israel.

Trump has repeatedly claimed dominance over Iranian airspace and used maximalist rhetoric to suggest the US has won and that Iran’s military capability has been eliminated in an effort to calm markets and an American public that is strongly opposed to the war.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

“They have no anti-aircraft protection. They have no nothing. They don’t have anything,” Trump told reporters on Tuesday. “They’re not putting up a fight. They’re not even shooting at us, OK?”

Days later, on Friday, Iran shot down an American F-15E fighter jet, raising questions about Trump’s declaration of near-victory in the war the US and Israel launched some five weeks ago. An A-10 Warthog plane crashed in the Persian Gulf the same day, reportedly after being hit by enemy fire.

“This is just another indication that Iran has lots of cards that they continue to play,” said retired Brigadier General Steve Anderson. “Obviously that puts us at risk.”

Although analysts praised the work of the US military, they said the shooting cast doubt on Trump’s claim of air supremacy over the Middle Eastern country.

“I think he’s going to have a harder time now at least convincing the American people that Iran has been totally destroyed,” said Republican former US Representative Adam Kinzinger, a former Air Force pilot and longtime Trump critic.

“It’s definitely going to hurt him politically because it gives more fodder to those who say he went in there recklessly,” he said.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

In a primetime speech to the nation on Wednesday night, Trump said the US would conclude the war in two to three weeks. But he has vacillated between saying that the US was enjoying victory and would leave opening the Strait of Hormuz to other countries and threatening to bomb civilian sites — a war crime under the Geneva Convention — if Iran didn’t do so on its own.

“Never in the history of warfare has an enemy suffered such clear and devastating large-scale losses in a matter of weeks,” he said. “Our enemies are losing and America, as it has been for five years under my presidency, is winning, and now winning bigger than ever before.”

But continuing the contradictions that hav

title: Iranian security official warns of 'big surprise' for US-Israel amid escalating conflict
content:
The comments come as the conflict, now over a month old, continues to widen. Iran launched missiles and drones early Sunday targeting Israel and Kuwait, with both countries activating air defence systems in response.

He further criticised US President Donald Trump’s messaging, suggesting it was aimed at shaping perception rather than reflecting battlefield realities

Iran warns of a "big surprise" for US-Israeli aggressors soon

Missile, drone strikes hit Israel, Kuwait as tensions rise

Iran claims control over Strait of Hormuz, key energy route Did our AI summary help?

A senior Iranian security official has warned that Tehran is preparing a “big surprise” for what he described as US-Israeli aggressors, as hostilities intensify across the region following fresh missile and drone exchanges.

In remarks to Lebanon’s Al-Mayadeen television network, the official reportedly said Iran was advancing according to a defined strategy and a pre-determined list of objectives.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

“We have a big surprise for the US and the Israeli regime; it only takes a little time,” he said, adding that recent developments could work to Tehran’s advantage. “Of course, their action in southern Iran can turn time in our favor.”

The official dismissed Washington’s assertions about the flexibility of its military targets, calling such claims irrelevant. “This is a meaningless word in military affairs,” he said, arguing that the US “bank of targets is not precise,” while Iran, he maintained, is proceeding “exactly according to its plans and its bank of targets.”

He further criticised US President Donald Trump’s messaging, suggesting it was aimed at shaping perception rather than reflecting battlefield realities.

“Given the failure of US military operations against Iran… Trump has resorted to the media to compensate for the US weakness in the battlefield,” the official said, also mocking references to civilian infrastructure. “That is ridiculous.”

The comments come as the conflict, now over a month old, continues to widen. Iran launched missiles and drones early Sunday targeting Israel and Kuwait, with both countries activating air defence systems in response, reported AFP. The attacks followed a renewed ultimatum from Trump, who warned Tehran it had “48 hours before all Hell will reign (sic) down on them” if it failed to reach a deal.

Iranian military figures have rejected the warning in strong terms. General Ali Abdollahi Aliabadi described the ultimatum as a “helpless, nervous, unbalanced and stupid action,” cautioning in turn that “the gates of hell will open for you.”

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Strikes and counter-strikes have increasingly focused on economic and strategic sites. Iranian forces have also emphasized their control over the Strait of Hormuz, a key global energy route, while targeting what they describe as economic assets linked to US and Israeli interests in the Gulf.

The Iranian official

title: 'If you import third world, you become third world', says US President Trump
content:
The comment, part of Trump’s broader immigration rhetoric, came alongside a sharp ultimatum directed at Tehran.

US President Donald Trump

Trump issues ultimatum to Iran, warns of military consequences

Iran launches missiles at Israel and Kuwait, rejects US ultimatum

Strikes near Bushehr nuclear plant prompt Russian evacuation Did our AI summary help?

US President Donald Trump stirred controversy with a post on Truth Social declaring, “If you import The Third World, you become The Third World!”, a remark that he posted even as he escalated pressure on Iran with a stark military warning.

The comment, part of Trump’s broader immigration rhetoric, came alongside a sharp ultimatum directed at Tehran. Trump said Iran had 48 hours to negotiate or face consequences, writing, “Time is running out -- 48 hours before all Hell will reign (sic) down on them.” The warning followed an earlier demand linked to reopening the Strait of Hormuz, a key global energy route.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Within hours, Iran launched missiles and drones targeting Israel and Kuwait, with both nations activating air defence systems, according to AFP. The latest attacks are part of a broader confrontation that began over a month ago after US-Israeli strikes triggered retaliation from Tehran, steadily expanding into a region-wide conflict.

Rejecting the ultimatum, Iran’s central military command responded with equally sharp language. General Ali Abdollahi Aliabadi called Trump’s warning a “helpless, nervous, unbalanced and stupid action,” adding that “the gates of hell will open for you,” echoing the US president’s phrasing.

Trump later posted a video claiming significant damage in Tehran, stating, “Many of Iran's Military Leaders...are terminated, along with much else, with this massive strike in Tehran,” though he did not specify when the strike occurred.

Meanwhile, battlefield claims remain contested. Iranian authorities said they had downed US aircraft, including an F-15 and an A-10, with reports suggesting one crew member is still missing. Local officials indicated that search efforts involve both military personnel and civilians, describing the operation as “still ongoing,” and alleging that residents fired at US helicopters attempting to land.

Concerns have also mounted over strikes near sensitive infrastructure. An attack close to the Bushehr nuclear plant killed a guard, prompting Russia to evacuate workers and condemn the incident as “an evil deed.” Iran warned that continued targeting of the facility could lead to radioactive fallout affecting Gulf capitals.

Economic sites have increasingly become targets. US-Israeli strikes hit petrochemical and industrial facilities, while Iranian retaliation caused injuries and structural damage in Gulf states, including Bahrain and Dubai.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

As the conflict deepens, its ripple effects are being felt across global energy markets, with Iran maintaining pre

title: US-Israel-Iran war live: Israel strikes Beirut as Oman, Iran discuss Hormuz maritime security
content:
Netanyahu hails US rescue of downed airman, praises Trump

Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has praised the US operation that rescued a downed airman in Iran, calling it a reflection of shared values between the two nations.

“This rescue operation reinforces the sacred principle: no one is left behind,” Netanyahu said in a video message addressed to US President Donald Trump.

He added, “This is a shared value demonstrated time and time again in the history of both our countries,” while highlighting Israel’s own history of daring rescue missions.

Referring to his personal experience, Netanyahu said he understood the significance of the decision, noting his involvement in past operations and the loss of his brother in the Entebbe rescue.

title: Closing Bell: Stronger rupee lifts markets, Sensex up 185 pts, Nifty above 22,700 in volatile trade
content:
Sudeep Shah, Head - Technical and Derivatives Research at SBI Securities

Nifty opened with a gap down and slipped to an intraday low of 22183 before staging a strong recovery. The index rebounded sharply by 530 points from the day’s low to close at 22713, up 0.15%. During the session, Nifty briefly breached its prior swing low of 22,284 (formed on March 30) but managed to reclaim the level by the close, a sign of buying interest at lower levels.

However, despite the strong intraday recovery, the broader structure remains weak. The index continues to trade below its key short- and long-term moving averages, while the RSI remains below the 40 mark, indicating persistent bearish momentum. Unless Nifty decisively breaks and sustains above the 22950–23000 zone with strong follow-through, the overall trend is likely to remain under pressure.

On the sectoral front, Nifty IT ended the day as the top sectoral gainer with gains of 2.6%, followed by Nifty Realty, which ended the day with a modest gain of 1.07%. Nifty IT saw its best closing since 16th January, 2026. On the other hand, Nifty Pharma & Nifty Healthcare both ended the day with losses of around 0.85-0.95%. With regards to stocks, HCL Tech & Tech Mahindra ended up as top two gainers while Asian Paint & Eicher Motors ended with losses.

The Midcap index formed a hammer candle on the daily chart, which is typically considered a bullish reversal pattern. The long lower wick suggests strong demand emerging at lower levels, hinting at a potential pause in the recent decline. Similarly, the Small Cap index mirrored the midcap movement, forming a small-bodied candle with a long lower shadow. This indicates buying support at lower levels and suggests that downside may be getting cushioned in the near term.

However, the market breadth was weak as the advance-decline ratio was skewed in the favour of bears at day’s close. A total of 274 stocks out of the Nifty 500 universe ended in the red.

Nifty View

Going ahead, the immediate support for Nifty is placed in the 22450-22400 zone. Any sustainable move below this zone could result in Nifty extending its weakness towards 22200, followed by 22000 in the short term. On the upside, the zone of 22950–23000 zone is likely to act as a strong resistance.

Bank Nifty View

Bank Nifty also opened on a weak note but found strong support in the 50000–49900 zone. The index staged an impressive intraday recovery to close at 51549, up 0.19%. On the daily chart, Bank Nifty has formed a sizeable bullish candle with a long lower shadow, highlighting strong buying interest at lower levels. Notably, the last time the index rebounded from this support zone (April 9, 2025), it rallied nearly 12% over the next seven trading sessions, making this level crucial to watch in the near term.

For Bank Nifty, the immediate support is placed in the 51100–51000 zone. Any sustainable move below this zone could result in Bank Nifty extending its weakness towards 50500, followed by 5000

title: US Iran War News Highlights (April 2): Iran launches a strike on Oracle's data center in Dubai, claims IRGC
content:
US Iran War News Live: Key remarks by President Trump till now

US President Donald Trump said the military’s strategic objectives in Iran have been “largely met,” indicating the campaign is entering its final phase.

He said US forces will continue strikes for the next two to three weeks, adding that “all objectives will be met soon, very soon.”

Trump urged countries dependent on the Strait of Hormuz to take the lead in securing the critical energy route, signalling a shift in responsibility to regional and global stakeholders.

He reiterated that regime change in Iran “was not our goal,” framing US actions as focused on limiting Iran’s capabilities rather than altering its political system.

At the same time, he warned of sharper escalation if diplomacy fails, saying Iran could be pushed “back to the stone ages” if military operations intensify.

Trump said the US has so far avoided targeting Iran’s oil infrastructure but kept that option open, stating, “We have not hit their oil… but we could hit it. It would be gone.”

He also warned of potential strikes on critical infrastructure, saying, “If there is no deal, we are going to hit each and every one of their electric-generating plants very hard.”

Drawing a comparison to history, Trump said the current conflict has lasted just over a month, contrasting it with World Wars I and II which continued for years.

title: Gold, Silver Rates Today (Apr. 2) - Highlights: Gold slips 3.46% below Rs 1.49 lakh, silver sheds 6.72% below Rs 2.28 lakh; precious metals outlook
content:
IBJA rates: gold dips below Rs 1.50 lakh, silver down 6.19 percent; why is bullion down?

The Indian Bullion Jewellers Association pegged the standard price of 10 grams of gold at Rs 1,49,569, which is a 2.72 percent decline from the previous session close at Rs 1,45,507. Silver, too, declined 6.19 percent from Rs 2,39,483 to Rs 2,24,660 per kilogram at its 12:30 rate session. According to the Augmont report (Apr. 2), bullion witnessed significant profit-booking after reaching fresh two-week highs near $4,800 and $75, respectively, amid renewed escalation in the US–Iran conflict. The prolonged nature of the conflict, coupled with Donald Trump’s hawkish remarks, has reduced expectations of near-term de-escalation, thereby reinforcing the US Dollar’s dominance as a global reserve currency and exerting downward pressure on precious metals.

title: Crude oil rally intensifies: Brent surges over 6% past $107 as Trump escalates Iran warning
content:
The rally came after Trump warned of tougher action against Iran, including threats targeting electricity infrastructure

Markets remain on edge as tensions around the Strait of Hormuz persist, a key passageway for nearly 20% of global oil shipments, which has been effectively disrupted since fighting began on 28 February.

Crude oil surged past the $100-per-barrel level after remarks from Trump failed to calm global energy market fears over the Strait of Hormuz disruption.

Brent crude, which was trading near $100 before the address, extended its gains sharply during and after the speech, rising over 6% to $107.29 per barrel as volatility intensified and markets reacted to escalating geopolitical tensions.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

The rally came after Trump warned of tougher action against Iran, including threats targeting electricity infrastructure, while also linking ongoing attacks to a broader escalation of the conflict.

Markets remain on edge as tensions around the Strait of Hormuz persist, a key passageway for nearly 20% of global oil shipments, which has been effectively disrupted since fighting began on 28 February.

Threats to maritime traffic are escalating as the conflict intensifies across the region. Most recently on Wednesday, an oil tanker leased to QatarEnergy was struck by an Iranian cruise missile in Qatari waters, according to the country's defence ministry.

Trump claims Iran’s Navy 'gone', Air Force 'in ruins' in war update | Top quotes from his address

The head of the International Energy Agency warned on Wednesday that supply disruptions are expected to begin affecting Europe's economy from April, adding that the continent had so far been cushioned by pre-war cargo shipments.

Meanwhile, some investors said it would take time for oil flows to return to normal even if the war ends within Trump's timeframe, particularly given damage to energy infrastructure. Trump's team has also suggested that reopening the Strait of Hormuz, which handles about 20% of global crude shipments, may not be necessary to end the hostilities.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

"Each passing week increases the global economic costs of the Iran conflict," Tiffany Wilding, economist at Pacific Investment Management Co, told Bloomberg. She added that persistent disruptions could gradually build economic strain, eventually carrying recessionary risks for the global economy.

On April 1, RBI ‌barred banks from offering rupee non-deliverable forwards to resident and non-resident clients

Indian Rupee

Rupee opens 130 paise higher as RBI curbs volatility

RBI barred banks from offering rupee non-deliverable forwards

Brent crude hit $106 after Trump signaled Iran war escalation Did our AI summary help?

The rupee gained the most in over 12 years on April 2, a day after the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) introduced additional measures to curb volatility in the currency, even as banks continued to unwind their offshore long dollar positions.

The rupee advanced as much as 1.8 percent to 93.17 against the dollar — the most since September 2013 — as currency trading resumed after a two days.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

On April 1, RBI ‌barred banks from offering rupee non-deliverable forwards to resident and non-resident clients. It also said companies cannot re-book cancelled foreign exchange derivatives contracts.

“In the near term, this is likely to trigger unwinding of such positions, reducing artificial demand for dollars and providing support to the rupee, potentially leading to appreciation bias or at least enhanced stability,” Amit Pabari, managing director at CR Forex Advisory, said.

FY26 was the worst year for the rupee in more than a decade due to a combination of factors, such as the spike in Brent crude prices and persistent outflows from foreign investors, among others.

The currency crossed the psychological 95-dollar mark on March 30 briefly, even as the RBI moved to limit banks’ exposure to net open positions on the rupee.

Crude prices surged around 4 percent after US President Donald Trump, in his address to the nation, signalled escalation in the Iran war without offering a timeline to end the war, which is in its second month.

While he claimed that the United States had met its war goals, Trump suggested the conflict could escalate if Iranian leaders do not accept the US’s terms ​during negotiations, with strikes on Iran's energy and oil infrastructure possible. Brent crude zoomed to $106 a barrel after Trump’s address.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

"I can say tonight ‌that ⁠we are on track to complete all of America's military objectives shortly, very shortly. We're going to hit them extremely hard over the next two to three weeks. We're going to bring them back to the stone ages where they belong," Trump said.

(with agency inputs)

title: Shaking buildings, broken homes: Horrific videos show extensive damage as 7.4-magnitude earthquake hits Indonesia
content:
Indonesia lies along the Pacific Ring of Fire, a region known for intense seismic activity due to the interaction of multiple tectonic plates, resulting in frequent earthquakes and volcanic eruptions.

Horrific videos show extensive damage as 7.4-magnitude earthquake hits Indonesia

Magnitude 7.4 earthquake strikes off Indonesia's Ternate island

Tsunami waves reported, one fatality confirmed

Warnings issued for Indonesia, Malaysia, and the Philippines Did our AI summary help?

After an earthquake of magnitude 7.4 struck the Northern Molucca Sea off Indonesia's Ternate island on Thursday, videos showing the extensive damage that happened in the area have surfaced on social media.

The earthquake damaged buildings and also triggered Tsunami waves. One person also lost their life in the incident.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

In one of the videos that surfaced on social media, chaos erupted at a departmental store when employees had to flee the shop after violent shakes rocked Indonesia.

A magnitude 7.4 earthquake was recorded off the coast of #Indonesia by the #USGS. The quake, with its epicenter in the Moluccas Sea, triggered a #tsunami warning for Indonesia, #Malaysia, and the #Philippines. 🌊 Authorities are monitoring the situation. pic.twitter.com/7LWf5JTetV — Siraj Noorani (@sirajnoorani) April 2, 2026

In another video, several things could be seen falling and breaking as the earthquake intensified. Several other videos also showed damage on the ground.

🚨🇮🇩#BREAKING | NEWS ⚠️ Watch as a building violently shakes with objects falling onto the ground after a major 7.8⚡️Magnitude Earthquake has struck in Indonesia a tsunami warning was issued for Malaysia🇲🇾 and the Philippines🇵🇭 pic.twitter.com/wGGaTlRUJe — Todd Paron🇺🇸🇬🇷🎧👽 (@tparon) April 1, 2026

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

The United States Geological Survey reported aftershocks of magnitude as high as 5, and Indonesia's meteorology agency BMKG reported tsunami waves in West Halmahera at 0.3m high and Bitung at 0.2m high.
